steps.title: Recognize what the opening appositive requires
steps.content: "A government body officially known as the Althing," opens the sentence as an appositive noun phrase—a phrase that renames or identifies a noun. For this to work, the information after the comma must introduce a noun phrase referring to that same entity, followed by a complete predicate.

steps.title: Match the appositive to the subject noun phrase
steps.content: Choice B provides "Iceland's parliament is one of the oldest in the world, first meeting in 930 CE." Here, "Iceland's parliament" is the subject of the clause and names the same thing as "A government body officially known as the Althing." This creates a valid appositive structure where both noun phrases refer to Iceland's parliament, and the verb "is" applies to both. Choices A, C, and D either introduce different entities as the subject ("the world's oldest parliaments," "the first meeting," or "CE") or create mismatched structures where the opening appositive doesn't identify what follows. That matches choice B — B is correct.
